title: Focus on spread of knowledge: financial institutions
description: Read a passage and answer questions about cross-cultural transfers of knowledge in financial institutions.
topic_path: Focus on spread of knowledge: financial institutions

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question, read the following excerpt.

> "By comparison [to twelfth and thirteenth-century northern Italy], economic life in the Eastern world - in the Abbasid Caliphate or [Song] China - was far more advanced (...). To discover modern finance, Europe had to import it. In this, a crucial role was played by a young mathematician called Leonardo of Pisa, or Fibonacci. 

> The son of a Pisan customs official based in what is now Bejaia in Algeria, the young Fibonacci had immersed himself in what he called the ‘Indian method’ of mathematics, a combination of Indian and Arab insights. His introduction of these ideas was to revolutionize the way Europeans counted. Nowadays he is best remembered for the Fibonacci sequence of numbers … But the Fibonacci sequence as only one of many Eastern mathematical ideas introduced to Europe in his path-breaking book Liber Abaci, ‘The Book of Calculation’, which he published in 1202. In it, readers could find fractions explained, as well as the concept of present value...Most important of all was Fibonacci’s introduction of Hindu-Arabic numerals. He not only gave Europe the decimal system, which makes all kinds of calculation far easier than with Roman numerals; he also showed how it could be applied to commercial bookkeeping, to currency conversions and, crucially, to the calculation of interest." 


From Niall Ferguson, _The Ascent of Money: A Financial History of the World_ (New York: Penguin Press, 2008), 32-33. 



**What does Ferguson claim about the relative economic development of twelfth and thirteenth century Italy compared to Song China and the Abbasid Caliphate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Italy was a collection of small city-states; the Abbasid Caliphate and Song China were large, complex empires.

choices.content: Italy was far more economically developed
Italy was far less economically developed
These places were all equally economically developed
Italian ideas influenced the economic development of these places

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question, read the following excerpt.

> "By comparison [to twelfth and thirteenth-century northern Italy], economic life in the Eastern world - in the Abbasid Caliphate or [Song] China - was far more advanced (...). To discover modern finance, Europe had to import it. In this, a crucial role was played by a young mathematician called Leonardo of Pisa, or Fibonacci. 

> The son of a Pisan customs official based in what is now Bejaia in Algeria, the young Fibonacci had immersed himself in what he called the ‘Indian method’ of mathematics, a combination of Indian and Arab insights. His introduction of these ideas was to revolutionize the way Europeans counted. Nowadays he is best remembered for the Fibonacci sequence of numbers … But the Fibonacci sequence is only one of many Eastern mathematical ideas introduced to Europe in his path-breaking book Liber Abaci, ‘The Book of Calculation’, which he published in 1202. In it, readers could find fractions explained, as well as the concept of present value...Most important of all was Fibonacci’s introduction of Hindu-Arabic numerals. He not only gave Europe the decimal system, which makes all kinds of calculation far easier than with Roman numerals; he also showed how it could be applied to commercial bookkeeping, to currency conversions and, crucially, to the calculation of interest." 


From Niall Ferguson, _The Ascent of Money: A Financial History of the World_ (New York: Penguin Press, 2008), 32-33. 


**According to Ferguson, what did Fibonacci do that helped develop European finance?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Fibonacci was not focused on religious issues in his book.

choices.content: Fibonacci opened the first bank in Italy
Fibonacci took mathematical ideas developed in India and the Middle East and showed how they could be applied to business
Fibonacci successfully disproved the religious argument against usury

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question, read the following excerpt.

> "By comparison [to twelfth and thirteenth-century northern Italy], economic life in the Eastern world - in the Abbasid Caliphate or [Song] China - was far more advanced (...). To discover modern finance, Europe had to import it. In this, a crucial role was played by a young mathematician called Leonardo of Pisa, or Fibonacci. 

> The son of a Pisan customs official based in what is now Bejaia in Algeria, the young Fibonacci had immersed himself in what he called the ‘Indian method’ of mathematics, a combination of Indian and Arab insights. His introduction of these ideas was to revolutionize the way Europeans counted. Nowadays he is best remembered for the Fibonacci sequence of numbers … But the Fibonacci sequence as only one of many Eastern mathematical ideas introduced to Europe in his path-breaking book Liber Abaci, ‘The Book of Calculation’, which he published in 1202. In it, readers could find fractions explained, as well as the concept of present value...Most important of all was Fibonacci’s introduction of Hindu-Arabic numerals. He not only gave Europe the decimal system, which makes all kinds of calculation far easier than with Roman numerals; he also showed how it could be applied to commercial bookkeeping, to currency conversions and, crucially, to the calculation of interest." 


From Niall Ferguson, _The Ascent of Money: A Financial History of the World_ (New York: Penguin Press, 2008), 32-33. 


**Which of the following best summarizes Ferguson’s argument in this passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Early banking practices predated Fibonacci’s book.

choices.content: Italian banking relied on the mathematical insights from other regions that Fibonacci brought together in _Liber Abaci_
Italian banking developed solely due to Fibonacci’s ideas
Italian banking copied the already established banking systems in the Middle East and China
Italian banking would have developed even without trade interactions with other regions
